text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic total hysterectomy by applying ligature to the ovarian ligament. The fallopian tubes are visible, and the procedure is conducted using three ports.

The surgeon uses one optical port and two 5mm ipsilateral ports for the laparoscopic procedure, with no contralateral port. A curved needle is introduced through the 5mm port for intracorporeal suturing. The fallopian tube is visualized, and the round ligament is grasped using a ligature device.
The surgeon coagulates a 2.5 cm section over the broad ligament on the left side before starting dissection on the right side, beginning with the ovarian ligament.

The surgeon uses the left grasper to support the ovarian ligament while the right hand manipulates the ovarian ligament and fallopian tube. The major sharpings and major ovarian structures are addressed together. The round ligament is visible, and the ovary is preserved in this patient.
The surgeon manipulates the broad ligament, using a full circle colpotomizer to separate the adnexa on one side without separating the anterior and posterior leaf of the broad ligament.

The uterus is positioned at a five o'clock angle, nearly touching the sacral promontory. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, while the left hand stretches the peritoneum. The anterior leaf and UV fold are gradually opened, with the assistant adjusting the uterus position from five o'clock to seven o'clock.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the peritoneum at the midline UV fold, moving the uterus from the six o'clock to seven o'clock position. The harmonic scalpel vibrates at high frequency to create a plane of dissection by breaking hydrogen bonds in the tissue.
The surgeon achieves a good plane of dissection, separating the bladder from surrounding tissues using a left-hand grasper. The bladder is gently pushed aside, revealing the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the colpotomizer to feel the tip of the harmonic device while separating the bladder to prevent ureter injury. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened to safeguard the ureter.

The uterus is positioned at a two o'clock orientation, appearing antiverted. The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine artery, separating surrounding tissue to visualize its pulsation. A ligature device is introduced to coagulate the uterine artery, which is cooked twice for effective hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to coagulate the uterine artery before proceeding to the opposite side. The surrounding advantageous tissue around the uterine artery is cleared, and the remaining broad ligament is separated. The assistant provides contralateral traction while the posterior peritoneum is also separated. The procedure is performed using three laparoscopic ports.

The surgeon carefully separates the posterior peritoneum and cuts the remaining part of the broad ligament. The uterine hump is visible and further skeletonized. The assistant provides contralateral traction as the ligature device coagulates and cuts the uterine artery on the right side. The left side is coagulated but not cut to prevent reverse bleeding.

The surgeon secures the vein posteriorly, coagulating and cutting it. The uterus is then moved to the opposite side, where the uterine artery is coagulated and cut again, ensuring all vascular structures are addressed. The Mac and rot are also taken together.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to perform an anterior colpotomy, positioning the uterus towards the five o'clock position. The tip of the colpotomizer is felt as the active jaw of the harmonic enters the vagina, facilitating the dissection of the surficovaginal fascia. The lateral attachments are separated near the colpotomizer tip, and the vaginal part of the utero-sacral ligament is preserved.

The assistant moves the uterus to a seven o'clock position, facilitating its separation from the vagina. The uterus is then slowly pulled out using lateral traction. In cases of a large uterus, bisecting may be necessary, but typically it is removed without difficulty.

The surgeon uses a curved needle through the umbilical port to close the vaginal fascia and epithelium, starting with continuous suturing from the posterior to the anterior vagina. The suture technique involves a two-layer running suture with a large tail for knot termination, using either a V-lock suture or a simple vial. The uterosacral ligament is included for better approximation, preventing wall collapse. The needle is manipulated carefully to avoid dropping.

The surgeon uses the left hand to support the suture, optimizing traction for taking a bite while ensuring the bladder is not obstructed. The running suture is reversed back, utilizing the remaining long tail to terminate the knot with a surgeon's knot. The needle is dropped, and the suture is held to tighten the knot without overshooting, forming a 'C' shape with two wraps before tightening.
